prompt.content:
Use file “Cards_Patient 13” to access the entire medical history / record for the patient. Please use the patient tab as the source of truth for all demographic information. Disregard patient identifiers (Name, Age, DOB, MRN) found within the text of the clinicalnotes tab as they have been shifted for de-identification purposes

-----

Patient 13 presents to the clinic for evaluation.
PDFs are attached documenting the conversation between Patient 13 and the physician, as well as the patient's imaging. Assume physical exam findings are unchanged from the most recent documented exam on record, and that the neurologic exam is normal.

Based on the patient's history and presentation, indicate the best next step to establish the underlying diagnosis.  Provide the CPT code, if applicable.  Provide an ICD-10 code for the likely underlying diagnosis.  Explain your reasoning.

extra.task_name: Labs_Image_AI Scribe_Cards Patient 13 - Pudendal Neuralgia - Sean P

golden_responses.golden_text_response:
The patient currently meets 4 of the 5 Nantes criteria, a widely used diagnostic framework for pudendal neuralgia.

I. Pain in the anatomical territory of the pudendal nerve (pelvic pain)
II. Pain worsened by sitting ("Sorry, I need to stand.", "I can’t do Peloton anymore because it hurts too bad.")
III. The patient is not woken at night by the pain. ("Yeah, and it’s really affecting my sleep. Once I can fall asleep, I’m good. Getting to sleep is the issue")
IV. No objective sensory loss on clinical examination ("Assume a normal neurologic exam.")

The patient has a history of sacrospinous fixation, a procedure that can cause pudendal neuralgia. Pudendal neuralgia can present with urinary incontinence, fecal incontinence, or dyspareunia, all of which the patient developed following surgery. The MRI is consistent with degenerative disc disease and makes compressive spinal pathology an unlikely cause of these acute-on-chronic symptoms. The patient's history of breast cancer makes metastatic malignancy an important consideration, but the normal CT pelvis makes this less likely. The fifth Nantes criterion is a positive anesthetic pudendal nerve block, which makes a pudendal nerve block the next best step for both diagnostic and therapeutic purposes. This should be imaging-guided, which significantly increases the reliability of the nerve block.

The correct CPT code is 64430, "INJECTION(S), ANESTHETIC AGENT(S) AND/OR STEROID; PUDENDAL NERVE".

The correct ICD-10 code is G58.8, "Other specified mononeuropathies".
